item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I heard a line on the radio the other day that reminded me of this article:

Figuratively, I love myself
But literally, I don't know that dude

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no real suggestion. The line is coming from an 18 year old after all, it is explainable by that.

I wanted to say also that SCPs like this are more than just something to upvote, because aside from being a good example of writing and a good executions of the format and all that, it is doing actual philosophical work. You are gaining ground here with regards to the development of the site and its components and I appreciate that very much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for reading and for the in-depth comment!

I think the only weakness I can see is the justification for attempting the pataphysical thaumaturgy ("16:28:03 | SCP-2758-A"). Personally, I think it reaches too far and may not be necessary in establishing a motive for the kids to perform SCP-2758, given the "rambling" in Addendum II.

I agreed with this and re-read both of those sections to see how I could edit it, but in reading it again, it looks like 16:28:03 addresses the 'how' and the conversation with Wardings addresses the 'why'. As in, it looks pretty evenly split now that I think about it. I'd be open to a suggested edit, but I'm not sure I'd know what to make.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is very entertaining to see your views on a recently-commented-upon 4k entry here articulated in such a constructive way, and that you have done it succinctly, in short time, and with such quality. It is the most enjoyable part of this article for me.

+ show block
– hide block

And there is plenty more to enjoy. You have simultaneously undermined pataphysics (16:27:38 Dr. Wickerford, Dr. Wardings: "Okay. What is crucial to understand is that") and given the program a quality installment: this is not self-masturbatory and avoids the common pitfalls of narrativic entanglement.

To show some love to under-loved aspects of the article: I love the first interview with SCP-2758-A where things are postponed in order to provided perceived medical needs, that really paints the Foundation in an admirable light and I don't think I've seen that little detail before; I love that when SCP-2758-A attempts to leaves the room, personnel can see cross sections of his body. The touch at the end…don't know if I'll fully understand it without spoilers, but…it is real nice.

I think the only weakness I can see is the justification for attempting the pataphysical thaumaturgy ("16:28:03 | SCP-2758-A"). Personally, I think it reaches too far and may not be necessary in establishing a motive for the kids to perform SCP-2758, given the "rambling" in Addendum II.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is my personal view on the subject, though not necessarily the exact view which Rimple holds and or held when he first began writing Overmeta. I'm by no means an exact authority on the matter, as, to be honest, it took hours of explaining to understand the entire point of what Rimple wanted to do with our article before it clicked for me. That said, I believe that the greater expanse of pataphysics is articles like this, where the intersection of our worlds, and the worlds we've created meet, and telling the stories intrinsic to such odd intersections, both big and small, and how real people are impacted by them, is exactly the kind of thing I was hoping to see come out of what we wrote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gotcha. Given that you're one of the OG Pataphysics people, I'll honor your viewpoint of it. Author post updated. And thanks for giving me more hope for Pataphysics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, where do I begin with this piece? This is well written, the attention to detail, in the containment procedures, description, and interviews is tight. The dialogue is human and well written, and SCP-2758-A's existential anguish from a bleak corporate controlled future and a desire to escape into a fictional universe that they've helped contribute and build pulled at the heartstrings. The careful and pragmatic consideration of some unstated code of ethics helped build the immersion and world in which this particular Foundation, a Foundation that is more humane, exists, and I appreciate that little detail quite a bit.

As one of the Authors of the most lambasted pataphysical article on the site, I found the shade thrown its way humorous. The depiction of pataphysics as being what is, this obtuse, and inaccessible approach to meta fiction, isn't what either Rimple or I intended when we wrote it, and it has been unfortunate, to say the least, that people have taken the specific style of that article, rather than the Foundation's attempt to manipulate reality and by proxy, Author's attempts and motivations in attempting to adjust the fictional canon to fit their personal headcanon, as the theme of Pataphysical articles.

Pataphysics isn't supposed to be about obscure technical language and terms, its supposed to be about the intersection of the infinitely many different worlds we create in the thousands of articles on the site, how the Foundation responds to this interaction, and how we, the authors respond to it. This falls within that purview.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, I guess that's one way to do a self-insert! Honestly, I was first worried that it might be a bit too bold for my taste but I really like the article, especially the reference to pataphysics and removing the phenomenon from its widely accepted place as a cosmic power of total control by virtue of creation and instead having it as a trivially known anomalous effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
cough
SCP-1548
cough

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. I edited the author post to reflect that I don't think it needs to be addressed when the Multi-U approach handles it fine, but I shouldn't have said that pataphysics outright doesn't exist when I knew about that Wikipedia page from other article discussions anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this. I still think getting caught up on the self-insert "wiki is real" trope to dismiss the actual concept of pataphysics is a missed opportunity, but I really like what this has to say about the urge to self-insert in the first place and about escapism in general. As always, I enjoyed the thoroughness you bring to the way the Foundation does things - my favorite detail is footnote 7, how exact they are and how the Foundation personnel themselves have to walk on eggshells.

+ note on pataphysics though
– hide block

I finally found the old page about this (linked above) that Hippo posted last year, which explains the cool parts of this better than I could.

One attempt at a definition might be to say that 'pataphysics is a branch of philosophy or science that examines imaginary phenomena that exist in a world beyond metaphysics; it is the science of imaginary solutions.

Non-figurative: Tom and Alice stood side by side in the lunch line.

Metaphor:
Tom and Alice stood side by side in the lunch line, two pieces positioned on a chessboard.

Pataphor: Tom took a step closer to Alice and made a date for Friday night, checkmating. Rudy was furious at losing to Margaret so easily and dumped the board on the rose-colored quilt, stomping downstairs.

We already use pataphors on the site all the time - for example, Humes are pretty much a metaphor for the level of reality in an area; having the characters build a de-hume-idifier and calling it a Reality Anchor is basically using a metaphorical solution to solve a metaphorical problem. I love that shit.

I'm put off by the circlejerkitude of "omg guys our writing is really real" too, but it only makes sense to me that the SCPF would in some realities have the insight to realize how much of what they're doing makes no sense in an existence that's not composed of narrative, and figure out how to turn the situation to their benefit (whether or not they're explicitly aware of why it works). I think there's a lot of potential there and wouldn't want to see the baby thrown out with the bathwater.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to say that this article resonated with something within myself. There is some deep, deep underlying personal commentary here, and I felt it. It's enough to make the article come alive in a sense, and I appreciated that a lot. Everything was mostly understood and it felt real and concise. Nicely done.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry you were confused. :( I'll try to help a little; here are some other articles that also use aetheric resonance imaging or similar: SCP-2696, SCP-3440, Transcript of a lecture given by Professor ████████████: Conclusion, Q and A, and Analysis of the Plokamisuchus.

Real-world comparison: an alpha radioscope is a device that allows the user to see radioactive particles striking a surface and causing a flash of light. I imagine aetheric resonance imaging as having a similar mechanism, but a machine detects the particle strikes instead of the human eye.

And this involves thaumaturgy, but not the department. IDK if I have an opinion on whether there's a thaumaturgy department or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: FYI i no-voted at the time but i slept on it and i decided i was probably just being petty, overall this is good enough to earn a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This thing is packed with so much jargon, large swaths of it make zero sense.

Aetheric resonance imaging

Like, what is that? This whole paragraph is like this, except it's more like the whole article is like this. I couldn't make it through the description.

I said I was gonna be okay with the Foundation having a thaumatology department, but I'm really struggling with that decision.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a sucker for meta in any sort of story, and this was well executed. Definitely reminiscent of Kaktus's 3812, although the existential anguish gives it a bit more of a personal touch that I'm sure a lot of this site's users can relate to - which is compounded by a generic protagonist that's easy to project onto. In the hands of a less skilled writer this would definitely feel like a shoehorned self-insert, instead of a sympathetic exploration of the wiki's importance in people's lives. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The one speaking line in which pataphysics is name-dropped is the only part that I wrote with that intention. It used to be more than one line but I changed it because I don't want that element to be over-the-top. I'll keep it in mind and see how people feel about it. Thank you for reading regardless and I'm glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: for the most part i enjoyed this but it definitely seems like parts of it were written specifically to take potshots at pataphysics and it hurts the work overall (though admittedly i might just be sensitive to it because i really like pataphysics stuff)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's fine to feel that way, but just for clarification: There is literally only one skip on the entire site that refers to any sort of 'thaumaturgy department' (SCP-3894).

Notably, this skip makes no such reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I do love Pataphysics being made fun of, I honestly can't get behind this skip because I'm sick of humes and the thaumaturgy department being everywhere.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I dig this basic concept — and the shade thrown at pataphysics was fun (and, at this point, likely well-deserved). It also puts a fun twist on the concept of 'author self-insert' — and I liked the Fold Selection Continuity Error.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this understated, blasé approach to pataphysics. The way you dismiss and trivialise points that other pataphysics articles would place on a pedestal is so refreshing. The dialogue feels realistic and the characters could be real.

It is very bold, far too bold for some people I imagine, but I'm able to enjoy it nonetheless. Future pataphysics articles should look more like this. +1

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
It might be too bold. It's a Humes + meta + thaumaturgy triple-hit combo. But if you count the fact that I throw shade at Pataphysics in it, maybe that can deduct one and make it a two-hit combo. But I'm gonna swing regardless.

Thank you to TheWydness, Sly161, Varaxous, 9Volt, and Countless Skies for feedback.

why it's Series III
Because

The former 2758, called "Self-Containment Processes" (title changed to singular now), was a mess of an article IMO. It was +100 for being a really fancy format screw (it had .gif text 'n shit) and that's about it. It was super long and I just really Did Not Like It, from concept to execution all the way through.

Thus, this is taking the concept — "self-containment" — and completely running with it from there. The former one was not meta (it was an incorporeal hazard that affected humans) and did not involve a human as part of the anomaly.

Edit: There is no canon and all that, but I do want to make it clear that in my canon vision as the author, Pataphysics is not relevant here. All of those topics are covered under Multi-U in my eyes, and the explanation is what Wickerford says. Okay after what Bleep said here idek so I'll leave it open to interpretation.

Spoilers
Spoilers

The very last expunged line said by Wardings says "Several of your users — some of whom are rather prominent, as authors or moderators or otherwise — are in fact personnel from baseline reality assigned to monitor your site for legitimate information security threats and persons of interest." I ended up redacting it in the end because the "other method of discouragement" that Wardings mentions is the action of posting this document in our reality construct so that you we can read it and see what happened to this person, and the Foundation wouldn't want us to have that as a factual statement.

Also, I do wanna make sure people get that the skip isn't the author. I'm not going to specify who it could be because it's a user that doesn't exist for us and is just for the sake of the narrative. The meta implication re: author is that I'm the Foundation agent who posted this. But no roleplaying on the forums.
